rationale: The citation is discussed with specific quantitative findings (-2.5 vs -3.7 elasticity) rather than being only a background list item. The citing paper uses those results to argue that ignoring endogeneity can seriously bias policy conclusions, and to compare this severity with omitted-variable effects. This makes the cited study a meaningful empirical support for the citing paper's framing of model-specification risks, even though it is not the main theoretical base of the article.
relation_description: The citing paper uses Bijmolt et al. (2005) as concrete empirical evidence that methodological choices (especially endogeneity treatment, and also covariate inclusion) materially shift price elasticity estimates. These reported effect sizes support the citing paper's argument about the practical consequences of misspecification in marketing policy models.
